postCopy.instagram: Somewhere between Budapest and Bucharest, a diplomat is dead. The train hasn't stopped. The killer is still on board. Every passenger has a story — but only one is lying about all of it.
postCopy.tiktok: A murder on the Orient Express, a diplomat found dead, and a timeline that keeps collapsing the closer you look. Can you crack the case before the next station? This free murder mystery game will test everything. Daily deduction puzzle, solve the case before it's too late. #MysteryGame

marketing.imagePrompt: Interior of a 1934 Orient Express sleeping car at 2 AM, dimly lit by amber wall sconces, mahogany paneling gleaming, a figure slumped in burgundy velvet seat, snow-streaked window reflecting ghostly corridor, cigarette smoke curling in cold air, shadows deep and deliberate, chiaroscuro noir lighting, muted palette of oxblood and charcoal with brass accents, Art Deco geometric detailing on ceiling, sense of elegant dread, cinematic wide shot with shallow depth of field, painterly realism in the style of Edward Hopper meets 1930s travel poster.
marketing.animationPrompt: Slow push through the dark corridor of a sleeping car, flickering sconce light, the camera pauses at a half-open compartment door — a gloved hand visible on the armrest, then the door swings slowly shut. Single beat of silence. Train whistle in the dark.

rookie.title: The Budapest Sleeper
rookie.flavor: The Orient Express cuts through frozen countryside, hours from any station. At dawn, Compartment 7 is found locked from the inside — and Hungarian diplomat Árpád Vásárhelyi is very dead. The dining car falls silent. No one saw anything. Someone is lying.
rookie.emptyState: The train keeps moving. The killer doesn't.

items.name: Countess Elsa Moravec
items.detail: Hungarian aristocrat travelling to Bucharest on what she calls 'private business.'

items.name: Dr. Emil Strauss
items.detail: Vienna-based physician with a medical bag he refuses to open for inspection.

items.name: Viktor Renner
items.detail: A soft-spoken Swiss salesman whose order books are conspicuously blank.

items.name: Compartment 7
items.detail: The victim's private sleeper, locked from inside with the security bolt engaged.

items.name: The Dining Car
items.detail: Where passengers gathered for the late supper — the last time Vásárhelyi was seen alive.

items.name: The Corridor Junction
items.detail: The narrow crossing between carriages, unlit after midnight and unobserved.

items.name: Poisoned Brandy
items.detail: A decanter of Rémy Martin found in the compartment, one glass poured and partially consumed.

items.name: Silk Garrote
items.detail: A length of ivory silk cord, consistent with the ligature marks on the victim's neck.

items.name: Sedative Injection
items.detail: Evidence of a needle mark on the victim's forearm, suggesting incapacitation before death.

clues.heading: Ligature Marks
clues.text: The examining physician notes deep, even ligature marks around Vásárhelyi's neck — consistent with a narrow, smooth cord rather than a rope or wire. The pattern suggests the killer stood behind the victim.
clues.alone: Establishes that a cord or garrote was used, eliminating blunt instruments and poisons as primary cause of death.

links.insight: Combined with the ivory silk cord found in Countess Moravec's travel case, this establishes the garrote as the weapon and her possession as highly incriminating.

clues.heading: The Steward's Account
clues.text: The sleeping car steward, Milos, states he walked the corridor at 1:15 AM and saw nothing unusual. But he also admits he spent nearly twenty minutes in the staff vestibule smoking — a gap in his patrol that he initially failed to mention.
clues.alone: Establishes a twenty-minute unobserved window in the corridor between 1:00 and 1:20 AM.

links.insight: The steward's unobserved gap perfectly overlaps the window when Renner claims to have been in the dining car, placing both accounts in contradiction and confirming the corridor was unwatched during the killing.

clues.heading: The Crumpled Telegram
clues.text: Tucked beneath the victim's travel bag is a crumpled telegram received in Budapest: 'DO NOT SIGN. THEY KNOW. DESTROY THIS.' The sender's name is torn away, but the transmission stamp is from Geneva.
clues.alone: Establishes that Vásárhelyi was warned before boarding — someone wanted to prevent him from completing his mission.

links.insight: The Geneva transmission stamp on the telegram connects directly to Renner's Swiss origin and his cover as a salesman, suggesting he sent the warning and then boarded to ensure Vásárhelyi could not act on it.

clues.heading: Silk Cord in the Case
clues.text: A search of Countess Moravec's travelling case, conducted with her reluctant permission, reveals an ivory silk cord — one end cut cleanly, the other frayed. She claims it was a dressing gown tie. It is not.
clues.alone: Places a silk cord consistent with the murder weapon in Countess Moravec's possession, making her the primary suspect.

links.insight: Combined with the ligature marks confirming a silk garrote, Moravec's possession of the matching cord is direct physical evidence linking her to the murder weapon.

clues.heading: Renner's Claimed Alibi
clues.text: Viktor Renner insists he was in the dining car nursing a whisky from midnight until 2 AM. The barman confirms seeing him — but only until 1:05 AM, when he stepped away 'to stretch his legs.' Renner had claimed to be there continuously.
clues.alone: Exposes a gap in Renner's alibi between 1:05 AM and some undetermined later time, contradicting his stated whereabouts.

links.insight: Renner's alibi gap from 1:05 AM aligns precisely with the steward's unobserved corridor window, meaning both the approach to and from Compartment 7 could have been made without witness.

clues.heading: The Geneva Ledger Entry
clues.text: Among Vásárhelyi's documents is a handwritten ledger page listing payments to a contact named only 'RV' — initials that match Viktor Renner — totalling twelve thousand Swiss francs over six months, with the final payment marked 'final settlement pending.'
clues.alone: Establishes a financial relationship between Vásárhelyi and someone with the initials RV, pointing strongly toward Viktor Renner.

links.insight: The Geneva telegram and the payments to RV together confirm Renner was Vásárhelyi's Swiss contact — and that the double cross had been arranged before the train departed Budapest.

clues.heading: Time of Death
clues.text: The physician estimates time of death between 1:00 and 1:30 AM, based on body temperature and lividity. The train passed through a scheduled slow zone near Debrecen at 1:10 AM — the vibration would have masked any struggle.
clues.alone: Narrows the killing to a thirty-minute window and explains how a physical struggle went unheard by neighbouring compartments.

links.insight: The Debrecen slow zone at 1:10 AM overlaps with the exact gap in both the steward's patrol and Renner's alibi, confirming the killer acted during this window of opportunity.

clues.heading: The Inside Bolt
clues.text: Compartment 7 was found bolted from the inside — yet the bolt mechanism is a simple lever type that can be tripped by a thin implement passed through the door gap. A letter-opener found on the corridor floor near the compartment fits the gap precisely.
clues.alone: Proves the locked-room appearance was staged: the bolt was engaged from outside using the letter-opener, eliminating suicide and making this deliberate murder.

links.insight: The staged locked room combined with the narrow time-of-death window proves the killer had specific knowledge of the patrol schedule and acted with deliberate precision during the Debrecen slow zone.

rookie.epilogue: Countess Elsa Moravec is detained at the Romanian border. The ivory silk cord, matched precisely to the ligature marks, is the evidence she cannot explain away. Under questioning, the full picture emerges: Moravec was not travelling on private business — she was a contract operative hired by a Bucharest faction desperate to prevent Vásárhelyi from signing a trade accord that would have undercut their interests. Viktor Renner, for his part, was genuinely Vásárhelyi's Swiss financial contact — the ledger entries were legitimate consulting payments, and his gap in the dining car was nothing more than a nervous man seeking fresh air. The telegram was sent by a third party Renner never identified. Dr. Strauss's sealed medical bag contained nothing more scandalous than a bottle of schnapps and a patient's private correspondence — he was treating a minor political figure incognito and feared exposure. The letter-opener that tripped the bolt was Moravec's own, left in haste. She had planned to retrieve it; the train moved faster than she anticipated.

detective.title: The Budapest Sleeper
detective.flavor: The Orient Express cuts through frozen countryside, hours from any station. At dawn, Compartment 7 is found locked from the inside — and Hungarian diplomat Árpád Vásárhelyi is very dead. The dining car falls silent. No one saw anything. Someone is lying — and the timeline is beginning to collapse.
detective.emptyState: The train keeps moving. The killer doesn't.

items.name: Countess Elsa Moravec
items.detail: Hungarian aristocrat travelling to Bucharest on what she calls 'private business.'

items.name: Dr. Emil Strauss
items.detail: Vienna-based physician with a medical bag he refuses to open for inspection.

items.name: Viktor Renner
items.detail: A soft-spoken Swiss salesman whose order books are conspicuously blank.

items.name: Lena Fehér
items.detail: A young Budapest concert violinist booked into the compartment directly adjacent to the victim's.

items.name: Compartment 7
items.detail: The victim's private sleeper, locked from inside with the security bolt engaged.

items.name: The Dining Car
items.detail: Where passengers gathered for the late supper — the last time Vásárhelyi was seen alive.

items.name: The Corridor Junction
items.detail: The narrow crossing between carriages, unlit after midnight and unobserved.

items.name: The Staff Vestibule
items.detail: The rear vestibule where the steward took his smoking break — the only unmonitored space on the train.

items.name: Ivory Silk Garrote
items.detail: A length of ivory silk cord, one end cut, consistent with the ligature marks on the victim's neck.

items.name: Poisoned Brandy Decanter
items.detail: A decanter of Rémy Martin found in the compartment, one glass poured and partially consumed.

items.name: Sedative Syringe
items.detail: Evidence of a needle mark on the victim's forearm, suggesting incapacitation before death.

items.name: Weighted Cane Handle
items.detail: A heavy silver-topped walking cane found in the overhead rack, with a hairline crack in the handle consistent with impact.

items.name: Before 12:30 AM
items.detail: During the late supper service — all passengers accounted for in the dining car.

items.name: 12:30 AM – 1:00 AM
items.detail: The period after supper when passengers retired — steward still active in the corridor.

items.name: 1:00 AM – 1:30 AM
items.detail: The critical window: steward's gap, Renner's alibi break, and the Debrecen slow zone all overlap here.

items.name: After 1:30 AM
items.detail: Body temperature indicates death had already occurred before this point — the corridor was quiet and observed.

clues.heading: Ligature Marks — Precision
clues.text: The physician's closer examination reveals the ligature marks are perfectly even in depth — suggesting the garrote was applied with practiced technique, not panicked improvisation. The angle of application indicates the killer was approximately the same height as the victim.
clues.alone: Establishes the killing was deliberate and skilled, not opportunistic — and that the killer's height matched Vásárhelyi's approximately 5'9" frame.

links.insight: Combined with the cord found in Moravec's case and her noted height of 5'8", both the physical evidence and the perpetrator's stature align precisely to incriminate the Countess.

clues.heading: Fehér Hears Nothing
clues.text: Lena Fehér, in the adjacent Compartment 6, states she was awake until nearly 2 AM due to anxiety before a Bucharest performance. She heard no raised voices, no struggle, no sound from Compartment 7 — only the train slowing briefly near what she later learned was Debrecen.
clues.alone: Confirms no audible struggle occurred, meaning either the victim was incapacitated first or the killing was swift and silent — consistent with garrotting from behind.

links.insight: Fehér's statement that the train slowed near Debrecen exactly when the killing must have occurred confirms the killer timed the attack to mask any vibration or movement during the commission.

clues.heading: The Crumpled Telegram
clues.text: Tucked beneath the victim's travel bag is a crumpled telegram received in Budapest: 'DO NOT SIGN. THEY KNOW. DESTROY THIS.' The sender's name is torn away, but the transmission stamp is from Geneva.
clues.alone: Establishes Vásárhelyi was warned before boarding — someone wanted to prevent him from completing a signing.

links.insight: The Geneva stamp on the telegram connects to Renner's declared Swiss base of operations, suggesting the warning and the killer originated from the same network that employed him.

clues.heading: Silk Cord in the Case
clues.text: A search of Countess Moravec's travelling case, conducted with her reluctant permission, reveals an ivory silk cord — one end cut cleanly, the other frayed. She claims it was a dressing gown tie. The cord's weave is identical to the ligature marks' width under the physician's magnifier.
clues.alone: Places a cord precisely matching the murder weapon in Moravec's possession, making her the primary material suspect.

links.insight: The cord's weave matching the ligature width, combined with the height-consistent attack angle, places the garrote and its owner in direct alignment with Moravec as perpetrator.

clues.heading: The Debrecen Slow Zone
clues.text: Train records confirm the Orient Express reduced speed to 15 km/h passing through the Debrecen maintenance zone at 1:08 AM, resuming normal speed at 1:22 AM — a fourteen-minute window of reduced vibration and engine noise.
clues.alone: Identifies a precise fourteen-minute window of reduced noise and vibration — ideal for committing a silent killing undetected.

links.insight: Fehér's silence from the adjacent compartment during this exact window confirms the attack took place during the Debrecen slow zone, when engine noise masked even careful movement in the corridor.

clues.heading: The Geneva Ledger Entry
clues.text: Among Vásárhelyi's documents is a handwritten ledger page listing payments to 'RV' — initials matching Viktor Renner — totalling twelve thousand Swiss francs. The final entry reads: 'final settlement pending — subject to completion of accord.'
clues.alone: Establishes a financial relationship between Vásárhelyi and someone with initials RV, pointing toward Renner as a paid contact.

links.insight: The Geneva telegram and the RV payments together confirm Renner was embedded in Vásárhelyi's network — but the double cross motif suggests Renner's loyalties were already sold to the opposing faction before departure.

clues.heading: Moravec's Claimed Retirement
clues.text: Countess Moravec states she retired to her compartment, Compartment 8, at 12:40 AM and did not leave until morning. However, the steward recalls seeing a female figure in a dark travelling coat at the corridor junction at approximately 1:05 AM — before his smoking break.
clues.alone: Places a woman in a dark coat at the corridor junction at 1:05 AM, directly contradicting Moravec's stated alibi.

links.insight: The dark travelling coat the steward spotted at 1:05 AM matches precisely the charcoal wool coat found hanging in Moravec's compartment, directly refuting her claimed retirement and placing her in the corridor.

clues.heading: The Charcoal Coat
clues.text: Hanging in Compartment 8 is a charcoal wool travelling coat — Moravec's. On close inspection, a faint smear of pomade is visible on the left lapel, at approximately neck height. Vásárhelyi was known to use a strongly scented Hungarian pomade.
clues.alone: The pomade transfer at neck height places Moravec in close physical proximity to Vásárhelyi — consistent with standing behind him and applying a garrote.

links.insight: The steward's sighting of a woman in a dark coat at 1:05 AM combined with the pomade transfer on Moravec's charcoal coat places her physically at the scene during the time of death.

detective.epilogue: Countess Elsa Moravec is detained at the Romanian border. The ivory silk cord, the pomade on her coat, the steward's sighting, and the collapsed timeline together build an irrefutable case. She was a contract operative hired by a Bucharest faction to prevent Vásárhelyi from signing a trade accord. She entered his compartment during the Debrecen slow zone, applied the garrote from behind, staged the locked-room bolt with a letter-opener, and returned to her compartment — all within the steward's twenty-minute smoking break. Viktor Renner's ledger entries were legitimate consulting payments; he was Vásárhelyi's genuine Swiss contact and had no part in the killing. Lena Fehér was exactly what she claimed: a nervous musician, hearing nothing because there was nothing to hear. Dr. Strauss's sealed bag held a patient's private correspondence, not poison. The double cross was elegant and cold — Moravec had accepted money from both the accord's supporters and its enemies, and chose the higher bidder on the night.

inspector.title: The Budapest Sleeper
inspector.flavor: The Orient Express cuts through frozen countryside, hours from any station. At dawn, Compartment 7 is found locked from the inside — and Hungarian diplomat Árpád Vásárhelyi is very dead. The timeline is a knot. The locked room is a lie. And at least two people on this train know more than they have said.
inspector.emptyState: The train keeps moving. The killer doesn't.

items.name: Countess Elsa Moravec
items.detail: Hungarian aristocrat travelling to Bucharest on what she calls 'private business.'

items.name: Dr. Emil Strauss
items.detail: Vienna-based physician with a medical bag he refuses to open for inspection.

items.name: Viktor Renner
items.detail: A soft-spoken Swiss salesman whose order books are conspicuously blank.

items.name: Lena Fehér
items.detail: A young Budapest concert violinist booked into the compartment directly adjacent to the victim's.

items.name: Milos Barta
items.detail: The sleeping car steward — diligent, nervous, and evasive about the precise timing of his break.

items.name: Compartment 7
items.detail: The victim's private sleeper, locked from inside with the security bolt engaged.

items.name: The Dining Car
items.detail: Where passengers gathered for the late supper — the last time Vásárhelyi was seen alive.

items.name: The Corridor Junction
items.detail: The narrow crossing between carriages, unlit after midnight and unobserved.

items.name: The Staff Vestibule
items.detail: The rear vestibule where the steward took his smoking break — the only unmonitored space on the train.

items.name: Compartment 8
items.detail: Moravec's sleeper, directly adjacent to the victim's — sharing a thin partition wall.

items.name: Ivory Silk Garrote
items.detail: A length of ivory silk cord, one end cut, consistent with the ligature marks on the victim's neck.

items.name: Poisoned Brandy Decanter
items.detail: A decanter of Rémy Martin found in the compartment, one glass poured and partially consumed.

items.name: Sedative Syringe
items.detail: Evidence of a needle mark on the victim's forearm, suggesting incapacitation before death.

items.name: Weighted Cane Handle
items.detail: A heavy silver-topped walking cane found in the overhead rack, with a hairline crack in the handle consistent with impact.

items.name: Cyanide Capsule Residue
items.detail: Trace analysis of the brandy glass reveals a faint bitter almond residue — characteristic of potassium cyanide dissolution.

items.name: Before 12:30 AM
items.detail: During the late supper service — all passengers accounted for in the dining car.

items.name: 12:30 AM – 1:00 AM
items.detail: The period after supper when passengers retired — steward still active in the corridor.

items.name: 1:00 AM – 1:30 AM
items.detail: The critical window: steward's gap, Renner's alibi break, and the Debrecen slow zone all overlap here.

items.name: After 1:30 AM
items.detail: Body temperature indicates death had already occurred before this point — the corridor was quiet and observed.

items.name: 12:00 AM – 12:30 AM
items.detail: The late supper's final service — passengers beginning to drift, bar staff distracted, corridor briefly unwatched.

items.name: Viktor Renner
items.detail: The Swiss contact with a ledger full of payments and a gap in his alibi no one can verify.

items.name: Milos Barta
items.detail: The steward whose smoking break created the only unobserved window — too conveniently timed.

items.name: Lena Fehér
items.detail: The violinist who heard nothing despite a paper-thin partition wall and claimed wakefulness.

items.name: Unknown Platform Contact
items.detail: A figure seen speaking to Moravec on the Budapest platform before departure — unidentified, not a passenger.

items.name: No Accomplice
items.detail: The killer acted entirely alone — the locked room, the timing, and the staging were a solo operation.

clues.heading: Dual Cause of Death
clues.text: The physician's full examination reveals two concurrent mechanisms: ligature strangulation and trace cyanide in the bloodstream — insufficient alone to kill quickly, but enough to disorient and prevent struggle. The garrote finished what the poison began. The cyanide was administered earlier, likely dissolved in the brandy.
clues.alone: Establishes a two-stage killing: cyanide first to incapacitate, garrote to kill — requiring either two people or one killer with prior access to the victim's compartment.

links.insight: The cyanide in the brandy and the garrote together demand a timeline where the killer or an accomplice had access to Compartment 7 both before and during the 1:00 to 1:30 AM window.

clues.heading: The Platform Figure
clues.text: A railway porter at Budapest Keleti recalls a man in a grey overcoat speaking quietly to a woman matching Moravec's description on the platform, minutes before departure. The man handed her a small package before walking away without boarding. The porter did not see his face.
clues.alone: Establishes that Moravec received a package from an unidentified non-passenger at Budapest, immediately before the train departed.

links.insight: The package handed to Moravec at Budapest, combined with the cyanide capsule residue found in the brandy glass, identifies the package's likely contents and establishes that the poisoning was planned before departure.

clues.heading: Barta's Station Log
clues.text: The steward's official station log shows his smoking break recorded as 1:10 AM to 1:30 AM. But the log itself is written in a single hand with uniform ink density — suggesting it was completed in one sitting after the fact rather than incrementally during the shift.
clues.alone: Suggests the steward's log was falsified or backdated, meaning his claimed alibi window cannot be trusted as contemporaneous record.

links.insight: A falsified log combined with Barta's evasiveness about exact timing suggests he was not simply negligent — his smoking break may have been arranged or extended deliberately to create the corridor gap.

clues.heading: When Was the Brandy Poured?
clues.text: The brandy decanter was half-full when found. Vásárhelyi's valet states the decanter was full and sealed when loaded at Budapest — and that the victim never drank brandy before midnight. The single glass was poured between 11:45 PM and 12:20 AM, before the supper crowd had fully dispersed.
clues.alone: Establishes the brandy — and therefore the cyanide — was administered during the pre-midnight window, an hour before the garrote was applied.

links.insight: The early brandy poisoning combined with the later garrotting confirms two distinct attacks, requiring either an accomplice or a killer who had undetected access to Compartment 7 before 12:30 AM.

clues.heading: Fehér's Partition Knowledge
clues.text: Under extended questioning, Lena Fehér admits the partition wall between Compartments 6 and 7 transmits sound clearly — she had complained to Barta about it the previous night. Yet she claims to have heard nothing from Compartment 7 after 12:30 AM, including no sound of the bolt being operated.
clues.alone: Fehér's admission about the partition's conductivity makes her claim to have heard nothing deeply suspect — either she was absent from her compartment, or she is lying about what she heard.

links.insight: Fehér's false claim of hearing nothing through a demonstrably thin partition, combined with the audible bolt click established in I8, points to Fehér as a knowing witness who chose deliberate silence.

clues.heading: Cyanide Capsule Wrapper
clues.text: Between Compartment 7's seat cushion and the wall, investigators find a torn wax-paper wrapper — the kind used to contain a single-dose cyanide capsule in intelligence tradecraft. It carries no fingerprints. Its size matches the small package handed to Moravec on the platform.
clues.alone: Establishes the cyanide was introduced via a pre-prepared capsule, consistent with intelligence-community methods, not improvised poisoning.

links.insight: The capsule wrapper matching the platform package's described dimensions confirms the poison was sourced externally before departure and delivered to Moravec by the unidentified grey-coated contact.

clues.heading: Barta's Bucharest Connection
clues.text: A cross-check of railway records reveals Milos Barta has worked the Budapest-Bucharest sleeper car seventeen times in the past year — and has received three wire payments from a Bucharest account traceable to the same faction Moravec was employed by. The payments were made the day after each run.
clues.alone: Establishes Barta as a paid asset of the Bucharest faction, receiving money for services rendered after each eastbound journey.

links.insight: Barta's falsified log and his Bucharest payments together prove he deliberately created the corridor gap on instruction — making him a knowing accomplice who cleared the way for Moravec.

clues.heading: The Bolt Timing
clues.text: The letter-opener recovered from the corridor floor shows traces of lacquer matching Compartment 7's door frame — consistent with it being used to engage the inside bolt from the corridor. However, the bolt mechanism produces a distinctive click audible in adjacent compartments. Someone in Compartment 6 would have heard it.
clues.alone: Confirms the locked room was staged from outside, and that the clicking bolt was audible to anyone in Compartment 6 — meaning Fehér either heard it and said nothing, or was absent.

links.insight: Fehér's acknowledged knowledge of the partition's sound conductivity and her silence about hearing the bolt click establishes she was complicit — either absent on instruction or deliberately withholding testimony.

inspector.epilogue: Countess Elsa Moravec committed the killing; Milos Barta made it possible. The Bucharest faction had planned the operation before the train left Budapest: a grey-coated handler delivered a cyanide capsule to Moravec on the platform, while Barta — a paid asset with seventeen prior runs in their service — was instructed to falsify his log and ensure his break covered the critical corridor window. Moravec entered Compartment 7 before 12:20 AM under some pretext and dissolved the capsule in Vásárhelyi's brandy; the cyanide worked slowly, producing confusion and weakness but not rapid death. At 1:08 AM, during the Debrecen slow zone, she returned, applied the garrote to the incapacitated diplomat, staged the bolt from outside with the letter-opener, and was back in Compartment 8 within minutes. Viktor Renner was genuinely Vásárhelyi's Swiss financial contact — the ledger payments were legitimate, his alibi gap innocent. Lena Fehér is the most troubling loose thread: she heard the bolt click and said nothing, but investigators find no evidence she was paid or coerced — her silence appears to have been the instinct of someone who understood, in 1934 Eastern Europe, that witnesses to such things do not flourish. Barta is arrested at the border. Moravec does not reach Bucharest.
